retrieved_docs.content: The following adverse effects have been associated with HIV medications:

retrieved_docs.title: HIV Antiretroviral Therapy -- Toxicity
retrieved_docs.content: Many HIV medicines have adverse effects that may require supportive treatment, monitoring, and adjustment of the HIV regimen. [23]

retrieved_docs.title: HIV Antiretroviral Therapy -- Adverse Effects
retrieved_docs.content: Postattachment inhibitors: Immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome

retrieved_docs.title: HIV and AIDS -- Toxicity and Adverse Effect Management
retrieved_docs.content: When prescribing antiretrovirals, a wide variety of potential drug-drug interactions, toxicities, and other adverse effects must be considered. Consultation with a pharmacologist is recommended.  Some common reactions are listed below.[NIH. HIV Guidelines 2023. ]

retrieved_docs.title: HIV Antiretroviral Therapy -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Ibalizumab: None

retrieved_docs.title: HIV Antiretroviral Therapy -- Toxicity
retrieved_docs.content: NRTIs: Mitochondrial toxicity associated with using NRTIs leads to myopathy, liver failure, and lactic acidosis. Lactic acidosis is fatal if not recognized. In patients with lactic acidosis, NRTIs must be immediately stopped, and clinicians should immediately administer supportive care. [30]

retrieved_docs.title: HIV and AIDS -- Toxicity and Adverse Effect Management -- Fusion Inhibitors
retrieved_docs.content: Enfurvitide, an injectable antiretroviral agent, can be associated with injection site reactions, including granuloma annulare and other symptomatic lesions. [90]

retrieved_docs.title: Preexposure Prophylaxis for HIV Prevention -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The WHO estimates that more than 630,000 people died from HIV-related causes in 2022.[WHO. HIV Data and Statistics. 2023] Thirty-nine million people are living with HIV, two-thirds of whom are in Africa. The CDC estimates that 1.2 million people in the United States had HIV at the end of 2021.[CDC. Basic Statistics. 2023] Long-term survival is possible with appropriate treatment but can come at a very high cost to the patient and healthcare system, particularly in resource-limited settings. [18] Those who survive, dependent on CD4+ count, may be at risk for new opportunistic or reactivated infections associated with high morbidity. [19] The use of highly active antiretroviral therapy can be associated with numerous adverse effects, drug interactions, and viral resistance to therapy. Many patients treated with protease inhibitors developed lipodystrophy and an increased risk for premature atherosclerosis. [20] [21] Older, non-nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTI) such as efavirenz are associated with neuropsychiatric and neurological manifestations. [22] Newer generation antiretroviral classes such as integrase inhibitors or tenofovir alafenamide have far fewer adverse events reported than previous agents and are generally well tolerated—but may be associated with metabolic effects such as weight gain. [23]

retrieved_docs.title: HIV Antiretroviral Therapy -- Toxicity
retrieved_docs.content: Efavirenz: Overdoses of efavirenz present with neuropsychiatric manifestations.

retrieved_docs.title: HIV Antiretroviral Therapy -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Raltegravir: None

retrieved_docs.title: Indinavir (Archived) -- Adverse Effects -- Most Common Adverse Reactions
retrieved_docs.content: Hemolytic anemia

retrieved_docs.title: HIV Antiretroviral Therapy -- Toxicity
retrieved_docs.content: Tenofovir: Long-term tenofovir is associated with nephrotoxicity and Fanconi syndrome and should be avoided in patients with preexisting renal impairment.

retrieved_docs.title: HIV and AIDS -- Toxicity and Adverse Effect Management
retrieved_docs.content: Didanosine and stavudine are no longer used due to severe adverse reactions and toxicity, such as fatal lactic acidosis, pancreatitis, and peripheral neuropathy. Lamivudine can rarely be associated with pancreatitis. [71] [72] [73] [74]

retrieved_docs.title: HIV Antiretroviral Therapy -- Adverse Effects
retrieved_docs.content: Fusion inhibitors: Injection site reactions, infection, trouble breathing, fever, blood in urine, dark-colored urine, low blood pressure, neutropenia, chills and shivering, and cough

retrieved_docs.title: Indinavir (Archived) -- Adverse Effects -- Most Common Adverse Reactions
retrieved_docs.content: Lipodystrophy

retrieved_docs.title: HIV Antiretroviral Therapy -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Etravirine: Hypersensitivity

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 1649
retrieved_docs.content: Non-nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs) constitute a class of antiretroviral drugs that inhibit HIV reverse transcriptase by binding to a distinct site on the enzyme, causing conformational changes that block viral DNA synthesis. Both efavirenz and etravirine are identified as members of the Non-nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors class, and nevirapin is also recognized as a member of this same class. Recognizing that efavirenz, etravirine, and nevirapin all belong to the Non-nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors group aids clinicians in selecting appropriate HIV treatment regimens and in anticipating class‑related efficacy and adverse‑effect profiles.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 670
retrieved_docs.content: Non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs) constitute an antiretroviral drug class that includes doravirine, delavirdin, efavirenz, etravirine and nevirapine. These agents share a common mechanism of inhibiting the reverse transcriptase enzyme of HIV, and they are grouped together because of their similar pharmacologic profile. Among them, doravirine has a specific drug‑interaction warning: it is contraindicated when used with rifapentine, a member of a class of rifamycin antibiotics, due to the risk of reduced efficacy or increased toxicity. Recognizing the class membership of these NNRTIs and the contraindication between doravirine and rifapentine is essential for safe prescribing and optimal management of patients receiving antiretroviral therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 1227
retrieved_docs.content: Nucleoside and nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors constitute a class of antiretroviral drugs that target the HIV reverse transcriptase enzyme, thereby blocking viral replication. This therapeutic class includes tenofovir disoproxil, emtricitabine, lamivudine, zidovudine, and dideoxycytidine (also known as zalcitabine). By sharing a common mechanism of action, these agents are frequently combined in HIV treatment regimens to achieve synergistic viral suppression and reduce the risk of resistance development.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2629
retrieved_docs.content: Non-nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors are a class of antiretroviral agents that act by binding to HIV reverse transcriptase and inhibiting viral replication. The drugs efavirenz, etravirine, nevirapin, and rilpivirine are all members of the non-nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor class. Recognizing that these agents belong to the same pharmacologic group helps clinicians understand their shared mechanism of action, anticipate class‑related effects, and make informed choices when selecting or switching antiretroviral therapy for patients living with HIV.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2156
retrieved_docs.content: Dolutegravir, elvitegravir, and raltegravir are all members of the class of integrase inhibitors that are used as systemic anti‑infective agents. As integrase inhibitors, these drugs target the HIV integrase enzyme, preventing the integration of viral DNA into the host genome and thereby forming a key component of antiretroviral therapy for HIV infection. Recognizing that each of these agents belongs to the same pharmacologic class helps clinicians select appropriate regimens, understand class‑related efficacy and safety profiles, and manage drug interactions in the care of patients living with HIV.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 1405
retrieved_docs.content: Delavirdin, efavirenz, and etravirine are all classified as non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs). As NNRTIs, these agents bind to a distinct site on the HIV‑1 reverse transcriptase enzyme, inducing conformational changes that inhibit viral DNA synthesis. By sharing this mechanism of action, delavirdin, efavirenz, and etravirine are employed in antiretroviral therapy regimens for HIV infection, often combined with nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors and other drug classes to achieve maximal viral suppression and reduce the risk of resistance. Their common membership in the NNRTI class highlights their pharmacologic similarity and guides clinicians in selecting appropriate regimens based on efficacy, side‑effect profiles, and resistance patterns.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 2 -- Community 1216
retrieved_docs.content: Nucleoside and nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors constitute a class of antiretroviral agents that target the HIV reverse transcriptase enzyme, thereby blocking viral replication. Tenofovir disoproxil, abacavir, dideoxycytidine, emtricitabine, and stavudine are all members of this nucleoside and nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitor class. By incorporating these drugs into combination antiretroviral therapy, clinicians can achieve more effective suppression of HIV, improve patient outcomes, and reduce the risk of resistance development.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1569
retrieved_docs.content: Doravirine, a non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor used in antiretroviral therapy, is contraindicated when co‑administered with rifapentine, a rifamycin antibiotic employed for tuberculosis treatment and prophylaxis; the interaction between these agents can significantly reduce doravirine exposure, making simultaneous use unsafe.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1685
retrieved_docs.content: Non-nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs) are a class of antiretroviral drugs that inhibit the HIV reverse transcriptase enzyme by binding to a site distinct from the active site, thereby preventing viral replication. The NNRTI class includes the agents delavirdin, etravirine, and nevirapin, each of which shares this mechanism of action while differing in pharmacokinetic properties, resistance profiles, and clinical use. Delavirdin was one of the earlier NNRTIs and is administered three times daily, whereas etravirine is a newer, more potent NNRTI often used in treatment‑experienced patients due to its activity against certain resistant HIV strains. Nevirapin (commonly known as nevirapine) is widely used in both adult and pediatric regimens and is notable for its long half‑life and potential for hypersensitivity reactions. Recognizing that delavirdin, etravirine, and nevirapin all belong to the Non-nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor class helps clinicians select appropriate therapy, anticipate cross‑resistance, and manage side‑effect profiles in the comprehensive care of individuals living with HIV.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 105
retrieved_docs.content: HIV infection is primarily transmitted through shared needle use, and its prevention and treatment rely on a broad spectrum of antiretroviral agents that target the viral reverse transcriptase enzyme. Nucleoside and nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NRTIs) – including tenofovir disoproxil, emtricitabine (also referenced as emtricitabin), lamivudine (lamivudin), stavudine (stavudin), zidovudine (zidovudin), and dideoxycytidine – act as nucleoside analogues that are incorporated into viral DNA and cause premature chain termination, forming a core component of combination HIV therapy. Non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs) – doravirine, delavirdine (and its mesylate formulation), efavirenz, etravirine, nevirapine, and rilpivirine – bind to a distinct site on reverse transcriptase to inhibit viral replication. Doravirine has a specific contraindication when co‑administered with the rifamycin antibiotic rifapentine, reflecting a clinically important drug‑class interaction. Understanding the class membership, mechanisms of action, preventive potential, and contraindications of these NRTI and NNRTI agents is essential for optimizing patient care, selecting effective regimens, managing drug interactions, and reducing the spread of HIV.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 90
retrieved_docs.content: HIV infection is strongly associated with shared‑needle use, which constitutes a major route of viral transmission. A broad spectrum of antiretroviral agents has been identified for both prevention (pre‑exposure prophylaxis) and treatment of HIV infection. Preventive activity has been reported for delavirdin, delavirdin‑mesylate, dolutegravir, efavirenz, elvitegravir, emtricitabine, etravirine, lamivudine, nevirapine, raltegravir, rilpivirine, stavudine, zidovudine and tenofovir disoproxil, whereas cabotegravir is highlighted for its therapeutic effect in established infection. These drugs belong to three principal pharmacologic classes. Non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs) – delavirdin, rilpivirine, efavirenz, etravirine and nevirapine – bind directly to the HIV reverse transcriptase enzyme and inhibit viral replication. Nucleoside and nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NRTIs) – dideoxycytidine, emtricitabine, lamivudine, stavudine, zidovudine and tenofovir disoproxil – require intracellular phosphorylation and act as chain terminators of reverse transcription. Integrase inhibitors – cabotegravir, dolutegravir, elvitegravir and raltegravir – target the HIV integrase enzyme, blocking integration of viral DNA into the host genome. Understanding the transmission risk posed by shared‑needle use together with the preventive and therapeutic properties of these NNRTIs, NRTIs and integrase inhibitors, and their class‑specific mechanisms, informs comprehensive strategies for HIV prevention, combination antiretroviral therapy, resistance mitigation and optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 277
retrieved_docs.content: Nucleoside and nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NRTIs) are a class of antiretroviral agents that target the HIV reverse transcriptase enzyme, thereby preventing viral DNA synthesis and replication. This drug class includes tenofovir disoproxil, dideoxycytidine, emtricitabine, lamivudine, stavudine, and zidovudine, each of which functions as a nucleoside or nucleotide analogue that is incorporated into the growing viral DNA chain and causes termination. Recognizing that these specific medications are members of the NRTI class is essential for constructing effective combination antiretroviral regimens, optimizing therapeutic outcomes, and managing class‑related toxicities in patients living with HIV.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1228
retrieved_docs.content: Delavirdin, efavirenz and nevirapin are all members of the pharmacologic class known as non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs). NNRTIs act by binding to a specific allosteric site on the HIV reverse transcriptase enzyme, inducing conformational changes that inhibit viral DNA synthesis and thereby suppress HIV replication. Recognizing that delavirdin, efavirenz and nevirapin belong to this same drug class is important for clinicians because agents within the NNRTI class share similar mechanisms of action, resistance patterns, and potential side‑effect profiles, which influences the selection and management of antiretroviral therapy in patients living with HIV.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1732
retrieved_docs.content: Fosamprenavir, amprenavir, and tipranavir are all classified as protease inhibitors that belong to the group of direct‑acting antiviral agents. As protease inhibitors, each drug specifically targets viral protease enzymes, a critical step in the replication cycle of viruses such as HIV, thereby providing a mechanism of action that directly interferes with viral maturation. Their shared categorization as direct‑acting antivirals highlights that they act on viral components rather than host cellular processes, which informs clinicians about common therapeutic objectives, potential for cross‑resistance, and similar considerations regarding drug‑drug interactions and adverse effect profiles in antiretroviral treatment regimens.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 1 -- Community 73
retrieved_docs.content: Direct‑acting antivirals (DAAs) encompass several pharmacologic classes used to treat viral infections, including HIV; within DAAs, cyclic amines such as rimantadin alter cellular synthetic activity, while integrase inhibitors, non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs) and nucleoside/nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NRTIs) constitute additional subclasses. NRTIs include abacavir (synonymous with dideoxyadenosine), dideoxycytidine, emtricitabine, lamivudine, stavudine, zidovudine, and tenofovir disoproxil, and are employed both for HIV prevention (e.g., abacavir, emtricitabine, lamivudine) and therapy. NNRTIs comprise delavirdin, delavirdin‑mesylate, efavirenz, etravirine, nevirapine, doravirine, rilpivirine, and raltegravir‑type agents that bind distinct sites on reverse transcriptase; doravirine is contraindicated with rifampicin and rilpivirine with pantoprazole due to drug‑drug interaction risks. Integrase inhibitors—dolutegravir, elvitegravir, raltegravir, cabotegravir, and fostemsavir—block the viral integrase enzyme required for proviral integration. Fixed‑dose combination regimens such as abacavir/dolutegravir/lamivudine and abacavir/lamivudine (also representing dideoxyadenosine/lamivudine) are classified as “Antivirals for treatment of HIV infections, combinations” and exemplify DAA‑based HIV therapy. Antiviral combinations specifically formulated for HIV are themselves a type of DAA. Shared needle use is identified as a direct risk factor for HIV acquisition, whereas methotrexate sodium is contraindicated in patients with HIV infection, underscoring important safety considerations in this population. Together, these entities delineate the classification, mechanisms of action, therapeutic and preventive roles, combination formulations, nomenclature equivalences, and contraindications relevant to comprehensive HIV management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 54
retrieved_docs.content: Enfuvirtid, delavirdin‑mesilát, dolutegravir, elvitegravir and raltegravir are antiretroviral agents that may prevent HIV infection, indicating a role in pre‑exposure prophylaxis. Cabotegravir and fostemsavir are antiretroviral drugs that may be used to treat HIV infection, providing therapeutic options for individuals with established disease. Dolutegravir, elvitegravir, cabotegravir and raltegravir are classified as members of the Integrase Inhibitors, anti‑infectives for systematic use, reflecting their common mechanism of inhibiting the HIV integrase enzyme. HIV infection is associated with the shared use of needles, underscoring the importance of harm‑reduction strategies to prevent transmission. Together, these relationships highlight both preventive and treatment modalities within the integrase inhibitor class and emphasize a key behavioral risk factor for HIV acquisition.
